You mean...?
This conversation never happened. Nobody tried to talk you out of it, nobody told you what you can or can't do...
As for this late night snack... you took it from the shared fridge yourself, didn't you?
You've been tampering with the locks again. You just won't give up, will you?
Unfortunately, the area that the search parties are looking is at least five kilometers from here. Don't expect them to hear your distress signal.
Hmph...
At least eat something.
Malnutrition during the recovery process will increase the time needed for bones to mend by twenty percent, and the chance of complications by thirty-five percent.
It's not my intention to hurt you, or leave an outstanding warrior with a permanent disability.
Cut the crap!
I warn you, you'd better kill me now...
I suppose that's an option...

As I said, however, I have no intention of bringing harm to Kjerag, and I certainly feel no need to take innocent Kjerag lives.
Besides, it was a miracle that we survived that avalanche at all...
It was, in your people's words, 'Kjeragandr's protection'. Right?
Shouldn't that be a sign to a faithful follower of Kjeragandr to value her own life?
No intention of bringing harm to Kjerag? You've been spying for the Victorians and stealing Kjerag's military secrets!
Such an unfair accusation.
Do you really think a few battleships constitute Kjerag's most important secret?
You speak of your god's blessings all the time, but have you really thought about what your god is?
What are you talking about... Kjeragandr is not to be questioned or doubted—
That didn't resonate? Well... I guess the only reason I'm talking to you about this is because there's nothing to do here to pass the time.
Ah well, doesn't matter anyway.
If you want to pass the time, I've got something to ask you.
How did a Victorian spy work for Rhine Lab for so long?
Why are you so sure that I'm a Victorian spy, when I've been working at Rhine Lab for so long?
Well... if you really want to hear it, I could tell you a little story—
There was once a girl in Victoria, who was incredibly fascinated to know how everything worked, and she wanted to spend her whole life studying.
A dozen years later, she went to Columbia as visiting scholar from the Royal Victorian Academy of Sciences...
It was not the research foundations of Victoria that found her, but an intelligence agency.
From that day forth, the only thing she was allowed to be curious about was the secret documents of the Columbian government.
But her curiosity was not so easily stated. As she dug through Columbia's secrets, she found an opportunity to satisfy the very depths of her curiosity...
At the end of the day, though, there is little that separates Columbia and Victoria.
People fight over the slightest morsel of profit, the order that took so much hard work to build is now teetering on the brink of ruinous war...
Who has the time to gaze up at the heavens... or even down at the ground we stand on, of which we know so little about?
...I find it all a tedious bore.
Early morning, Hall of Writings, Vine-Bead Court.
Good thing the window isn't too high.
The clerics have gone to their morning prayers... the observation station is a sensitive topic right now. I need to avoid them.
Maps, geographical records... anything that can help...
The morning sun shines through the window, and falls gently on the aged books.
Elena dusts off the books in front of her, but finds the writing of the clerics difficult to decipher. She begins to regret not bringing a dictionary of the Kjeragi language.
How do they categorize this stuff? This one's... a collection of quotes?
'Kjeragandr, showed Herself, to me, and said—'
'Seek not my temple, but my heart.'
Whoa—!
Elena lets go in panic, and the heavy book hits the floor.
Through the bookshelves, she sees a surprised face under a hood, and a crossbow pointed at her face.
I think she's surrendering, Enya. What do we do?
Who's saying anything about surrendering?!
Wait a moment... Enya? Enya Silverash?
Y-You're the Saintess?
Shh—
Elena nods in realization.
Keep quiet, and we can talk. Alright?
Elena nods carefully.
Put away your crossbow, Joanna. Let's not scare our friend here.
But she sneaked into the Vine-Bead Court. Doesn't seem like something a good person would do.
Well, we sneaked in too...
Your attire... are you from Rhine Lab?
Yes! I'm Elena, a researcher in the Energy Section.
I'm sorry. I was just looking for a map of Kjerag, preferably recording the locations of mountains and Originium veins...
If I find that special vein, maybe I could—
...You could what?
Elena glances at the young woman before her, who looks about the same age as her, while being the undisputed sovereign of this country.
Elena is unsure if what she is about to say might offend her, but when she sees the pure curiosity on her face...
Maybe... I can explain how this Catastrophe happened!
You mean that Kjerag has been safe from Catastrophe for a thousand years because of a hidden Originium vein?
Yes! There is an undiscovered Originium vein in the mountains surrounding Kjerag, which created an energy field that prevented Catastrophes from forming.
Impossible! It's Kjeragandr that protects Kjerag!
Has anyone ever seen Kjeragandr with their own eyes?
I—
Many people have witnessed the miracles of Kjeragandr with their own eyes!
I respect your culture and traditions, but that doesn't prove anything.
Just because you see something that can't be explained right now doesn't prove the existence of some supernatural being behind it.
And how do you prove the veins have anything to do with it?
The interactions between Originium is extremely complex, and what we know is only the tip of the iceberg.
Rhine Lab has conducted a number of experiments in recent years.
Under certain conditions, Originium can form energy fields that cause atmospheric Originium particles to disperse rather than converge, preventing the formation of Catastrophes.
This is something that we have not yet been able to recreate under controlled conditions...

But if an Originium vein with the right conditions exists... then preventing Catastrophe may be a real possibility.
I've made detailed calculations—
Excuse me, can I borrow this map?

Elena takes out her notebook and show it to the other two. She goes on a long explanation about the formulas as she draws on the page, not paying attention to whether her listeners are able to keep up.
Finally, Elena confidently draws a circle at a location in the foothills on the map.
...Therefore, the Originium Vein Theory is the most likely explanation for Kjerag's lack of Catastrophes!
I'm quite sure that my calculations are accurate, and if we find an Originium vein at this location, it would explain everything!
Joanna is in a daze. She glances at Enya, hoping the Saintess will rebuke this blasphemy.
But the Saintess is quiet, biting her lower lip lightly.
(Whispers) Say something! I don't know what the hell she's writing!
How do you explain the recent Catastrophe, then?
Based on the data, I suspect something from beyond the Starpod fell on Mount Karlan the day before the Catastrophe.
Look! These are the data that the station observed the day before the Catastrophe. These are infrasound waves created by the impact of the extraterrestrial object.
Elena turns on her portable terminal, displaying a sharp break on the line graph.
This object must be massive in size and scale. I think the Catastrophe was caused by it falling onto the central area of the vein, causing a shift in the energy field!
There are reasons for all these things! It's not magic, or the work of a god!
Enya cannot comprehend what she is hearing. Her memory begins to blur, and she feels a sort of link being torn apart.
'Kjarr.'
A familiar voice, belonging to a friend, a miracle, or... a hallucination?
She can't believe it. She must not. Fear and loneliness comes over her, and it is all she could do to keep her arms from trembling.
Say something, Enya...
I... I can understand Elena's position.
However, I recall a section of the Kjeragandr...
'Her heart is the heart of Karlan, deep within the mountains. From the beating of Her heart grew the flesh and bones of Kjerag.'
This is the description of the origins of Kjerag, as the scriptures record.
We used to assume that the Heart of Karlan was Lake Silberneherze, which was why we built Kjeragandr's statue there.
In my studies these past several days, however, I found that the ancient tomes considered the freezing of the Heart of Karlan to be an unusual phenomenon. But Lake Silberneherze freezes every year...
Sounds like a figure of speech...
Don't interrupt the Saintess while she's thinking!
Maybe the Heart of Karlan actually refers to something else...
The Great Elder guided me to the 'Radsorn', but it is in fact the 'Heart of Karlan' that holds the answer...
You mentioned the central area of this 'special vein'?
Yes! I built a model based on the shape of Kjerag's mountains, and I calculated that the extraterrestrial object likely fell on...
this location.
Isn't this.... the Kjerag-Kazimierz mines?
Mines? Yes! Now it all makes sense!
There's no time to waste! We need to go—
One moment, foreigner! We didn't say anything about letting you around Kjerag on your own, not to mention all the disrespect you've shown to Kjeragandr—
It's alright, Joanna.

We don't force our faith onto others. Besides, we need to find the truth behind this Catastrophe... we'll let her travel with us.
Elena...
Hm?
While Enya and Joanna were talking, Elena put the books back on the shelf, and now sits on the window sill, waving at the other two.
Actually, there's usually no one at the main entrance to the Hall of Writings.
Cold moonlight pours through the dark clouds upon the decorations in Ratatos's room.
Sciurus thought she would be afraid to see Ratatos's things, but she feels so tired that she does not even have the strength for sorrow.
She knows full well that she simply imitated Ratatos in dealing with the other clans, a few days ago.
But the more she tries, the more Ratatos's shadow lingers.
Sciurus looks outside. It seems snow is once again falling on Browntail land.

lines.st: 'Look, Sciurus. Do this to the snowball, and it'll go even further when you throw it. Try it.'
'I don't need you to teach me...'
'I don't want to become you.'
'I hate you. I hate everything you make me do.'

lines.x: The echoes of memory are like the deafening roar of a mountain collapsing. She feels a lump in her chest.
The dust-covered contracts and letters in the box bear witness to the development of Clan Browntail over the years.
Sciurus examines the marks left behind by Ratatos on the family's business documents, and finds, at the bottom of each page, where the name of the Browntail matriarch should be—
'Sciurus'

lines.st: 'Remember, Sciurus.'
'I'm your older sister, and I'll become the leader of Clan Browntail. So you need to listen to me.'
'Prove that you can do better, if you're not happy about that.'
'When that time comes...'
'I hope you'll carry the future of Clan Browntail on your shoulders.'

lines.x: Sciurus anxiously flips through the pages.
But each and every document has her name on it.
Until she reaches the bottom of the pile, that little box from her memory—
The secret that belongs to the two of them.

lines.st: 'This holds all the things that I want to give you, but don't want the adults to see.'
'Don't tell anyone. It's a secret.'
'A secret between the two of us, you and me.'

lines.x: This is...
A communicator?
Sciurus gazes at the device in her hand, the dim light illuminating her hesitation.
The final audio log...
I got Karlan Trade's intel about the suspicious woman. I've been watching her all morning, and I think she's planning to make a run for it.
This voice... it's Monch?
Good. Keep a close eye on her. Don't let her get away, or pass the documents to anyone else.
Yes, ma'am... Karlan Trade says we need to be careful and not draw attention.
Take her alive, if possible. There are a lot of questions we need to ask her.
I'll tell Enciodes to send help from Karlan Trade.
Understood. I'll do my best.
Be careful, and don't leave loose ends.
Finally, there is an unanswered call from Karlan Trade.
Call time... yesterday?
...Karlan Trade?
How much longer?
Patience please, Mr. Bellingham.
As you know, the Duke has been very busy lately, having to receive several guests each day.
You should remember, sir, the last time I came here...
*sigh*——
So, I'd like to ask... How has she been lately?
Fairly pleasant for the most part. After all, the negotiations with the Kazimierzians have been nearing completion over the past few days.
As soon as he finishes speaking, a dejected gentleman strides past the Trilby Asher.
The Trilby Asher recognizes him as Derek, head of the press office. Perhaps due to the recent wave of bad publicity, he wipes the sweat off his brow with trembling hands.
...I suspect today is not one such day.
Is that Bellingham outside? Let him in first. Don't keep him waiting.
The butler nods, and the Trilby Asher hesitates for a moment before stepping into the room.
Seated in the middle is the Duke of Caster, with a young noble standing at her side.
...I have another question. What kind of person is this Kazimierzian representative you met?
He is a merchant, Your Grace.
Hmm, Kazimierz...
Tell him that should he agree to my terms, I am willing to lift some of the embargos on industrial equipment.
For them, there are many ways to obtain cheap minerals, and Kjerag is hardly irreplaceable.
I will let him know that only Victoria can satisfy his technological and equipment needs, especially when it comes to Originium—
No.
You are a member of House Caster. These minutiae are best left for servants to convey, whereas we should always leave ourselves room to maneuver.
The Kazimierzians know little about the resources we command, so why not let them exercise their imagination? Do you understand?
No worries. I guarantee that he will side with us.
'Forever honor your commitments.' If you cannot, don't make them in the first place.
That is true of the Kazimierzian representative, and even moreso for me.
You may leave.
Yes... My apologies, Your Grace.
Once again, you have to see me disciplining these good-for-nothing whelps.
Belli.
I thank Your Grace for the trust you have placed in me.
Let's not waste time on small talk. I trust you saw the gentleman who left earlier, yes?
If I'm not mistaken, that was a certain Derek, the man in charge of news and public opinion.
He should have reported to me that the negative press, be it about Kjerag or Victoria, was under control.

He was instead quite profuse with his complaints, going on and on about how he couldn't do it, that it would take too long to communicate with the film crew, that he wanted to trouble me to watch the footage they sent back.
If he feels like he hasn't been given enough time, he should've known that my patience has its limits too.
So, do you have any good news for me, Belli?
...Yes, Your Grace.
Our informant at the Rhine observatory provided very accurate intel—indeed, there is something highly suspicious about the mining site in which Kjerag is working with Kazimierz.
Following the clues she provided, my men successfully infiltrated the site and obtained these data tables.
Just point out the highlights.
Please look at this section, and the subsequent section on the next page...

The former represents the mine's daily output, whereas the latter represents the purchase volume, or export volume. Finally, the last page is the mine's financial report; pay particular attention to the net profitability figure. For example, in the last two months—
The difference is massive... This mining site has been operating at a loss, and the greater the extraction, the greater the losses.
Exactly. We even found this mine import record. Please take a look...
Hmm? A large mining site needs to separately import the very same minerals...?
Therefore, I believe this confirms the earlier theory.
This so-called joint mining project is actually Karlan Trade's arsenal in disguise.
And this is the trump card that has been flipped over? Sitting right there in a mining site shared jointly with a foreign company?
Which is why our agents, including myself, initially overlooked this area. It is hard to imagine that anyone could stomach such a risk.
Placing a piece in what normally would be a blind spot. It's very much his style.
However, you're still one move behind, Belli.
As difficult as it is to admit, when Wellington made his move, it became imperative for us to strengthen ourselves.
Given these circumstances, Kjerag is a valuable asset.
I will not accept a lose-lose scenario; we must ensure that Enciodes's military preparations do not catch us flat-footed.
Only by knowing what his trump card exactly is can we properly plan against Kjerag in advance.
Your Grace, if you wish to confront the Duke of Wellington...

Bringing Kjerag under the control of House Caster, as well as seizing their manpower and resources, is of utmost priority. I personally believe that we should conduct a comprehensive survey of this mining site.
I believe the Kazimierzians will accept your term, and it's only a matter of time until they withdraw from Kjerag.
As soon as we take control of this mining area, I will send our envoys there immediately.
Why not act before the paperwork is officially signed? We cannot give Karlan Trade the time to cover their tracks.
So, under the buyer's name... look for another excuse through the Vine-Bead Court?
Even if they suspect that something is amiss with the Kazimierzians, a hasty retreat would inevitably leave traces behind.
The Vine-Bead Court is ultimately an expendable asset. Due to your previous botch job, there is no way Enciodes would not have his eye on them.
Then, we'll squeeze every last drop of value from the Vine-Bead Court before Enciodes makes his move.
Is that truly the missing step, Belli? Be more economical, and think it through some more.

The current Great Elder has consistently turned down my goodwill, and given the inability to mobilize the full strength of the Vine-Bead Court, we must be more careful when accessing the feasibility of this plan.
Yet, Your Grace, he did not stop us from infiltrating the lower and middle ranks either.
Perhaps this is the Vine-Bead Court's persistent arrogance, ripe for us to take advantage.
Perhaps the allure of overthrowing Enciodes will prove to be a gift too irresistible for Kjerag's religious figures.
So, do you intend to—
You are free to move the pawns inside the Vine-Bead Court as you please, but you must find out exactly what is going on within the mining site.
Let us wait to see how Enciodes reacts. He might just give us another reason to get involved in Kjerag.
Yes, Your Grace.
Right, I'd like you to do me a favor.
Derek left some odds-and-ends behind. Go ahead and take them with you.
When it comes to our dealings with foreign nations, we always have some journalist friends who make too much noise.
Can I set the camera up right here?
Thank you... Let's get started if you're ready.

Venerable Great Elder Adso, thank you so much for agreeing to this interview. I sincerely hope you are willing to answer a few of my questions, as well as those of the countless others who want to know more about Kjerag.
First, I'd like to ask about something that many around Kjerag have been curious about recently.
The people of Kjerag broadly believe that Kjeragandr protects this land. In your opinion, how would you explain this Catastrophe? Does this mean that Kjerag has lost its divine protection?
That is correct. Kjeragandr indeed watches over this land.
However, She is not a tyrant who imposes harsh decrees, but rather a patient mentor and a guiding hand. Her protection of Kjerag is not as straightforward as many would imagine.
Her knowledge of the true way of life runs deep, and so She taught the people of Kjerag how to live in this land filled with calamity and devoid of thought.
Thus, the people of Kjerag came to have a soul, and the many calamities no longer disturbed them.
Even so, I am still very curious as to how a god who neither reveals its true form nor has performed any 'miracles' as proof of its divinity can earn a people's faith.
This belief seems to lack any grounding in evidence.
Hahaha... Guest from afar, I can see that you hide your arrogance behind polite words.
You try to reduce Kjerag's beliefs into a most basic form of logic, which you then criticize in an attempt to extol your own correctness and wisdom...
Please believe me... that was not my intent.
It's fine. Don't worry...
Then, may I ask you a question first? In your opinion, what kind of principles do the Victorians, who observe no religion, follow in their lives?
That's a simple answer— Victoria has her own laws.
Who drafted these laws? And who granted them the authority to do so?
If you're asking about the legitimacy of the Victorian regime, we need only look back at history to answer that question.
The land was originally occupied by Dracos until about three hundred years ago, when Aslans from Sargon launched a rebellion in Victoria, ultimately seizing control of the country.
Then, I take it that in your view, might makes right?
Aslans are strong, and therefore righteous. Dracos are weak, and therefore unjust. This so-called justice shifts just as easily as the balance of power in war.
By the same logic, the devils of Kazdel should feel no shame or remorse about invading the lands of other nations, their only sin being that they fell one step short of ultimate victory.
...That is a patently absurd conclusion.
Of course, of course... I doubt that any regime would hinge its legitimacy upon violence. Because, they know all too well that violence will inevitably be undone by greater violence.
Nothing can remain young and mighty forever—neither people, nor governments. Therefore, they must become compassionate, in hope that the same compassion will be afforded to them when they are weak.
So, let us return to that question... Why do the legal principles we take for granted, or rather, the virtues prevalent across the different civilizations sharing this land, exist in the first place?
To where should we direct our questions? Do we possess the 'sublimity' inherent to being human? Are our lives innately meaningful?
Apologies... but I am not well-versed in these subjects of philosophical speculation. May I ask, what is the point of these questions that seem so far-removed from our daily lives?
You mention life... In fact, as long as you seriously reflect upon your own life, you will very easily find that there is nobody on this great land who is entirely devoid of faith.
People always choose to place their faith in something, be it money, violence, knowledge, fame, philanthropy, or pleasure...
If a person genuinely believes that nothing is inherently true, then he will quickly find himself akin to a puppet with its strings cut, unable to move at all.
The followers of Kjeragandr seek the eternal solace of the mind by questioning ourselves and seeking out the traces of Her existence.
An interesting thought indeed... This seems quite different from the common perceptions floating around Kjerag.
Please tell me, what was your perception of Kjerag before you witnessed the human tragedy caused by Catastrophe?
'The last pure land on Terra'? 'A sanctuary protected by the gods?' 'A land of simple and kind, poor but happy people?'
You're quite well-versed in these touristy catch-phrases...
It was your people who discovered and abused the power of Originium, which ruined everything. You know that countless people drowned beneath the waves you stirred.
And it is this guilt that led you people to weave your illusions into the fantasy of Kjerag—a paradise where one can reap the benefits of Originium with none of the costs.
You imagine purity because you know filth. Innocence because you know deception. Magnanimity because you know opportunism. Piety because you know nihilism.
How, then, can you see the farmers who've left their homeland behind, the craftsmen whose unfinished goods pile up like a mountain, and the hunters who return home empty-handed?
You might even deny the existence of these people so that you may feel at ease when you swallow the blood and sweat of us 'savages' to 'purify your souls'.
...As an investigative journalist, it is my responsibility to spread the truth. And, I am hardly the only Victorian who cares about the truth regarding Kjerag.
Young lady, you promised to spread the truth about Kjerag across the lands. Yet your lenses are pointed at those who are more lost, more deserving of pity.
Do you think we should call this the truth? Or simply a fable?
I must admit, your questions have left me chagrined... Be it my colleagues or myself, it is hard to set down our morbid curiosity as onlookers.
All you need to know is that Kjerag's belongings are not playthings sitting behind the glass of a Victorian shop. Kjerag does not, and will not, need anyone's pity or charity.
Kjerag will always be the people of Kjeragandr—the home of the Kjerag people.
All right, I think I'm convinced now.
So, in your opinion, this Catastrophe will not shake the people's faith in Kjeragandr?
Hmm...
To be honest, Kjerag's faith is undergoing a serious test...
I cannot deny that this Catastrophe has sown the seeds of darkness in the hearts of the people, causing them to gradually turn to violence and selfishness, abandoning many of their former virtues...
But was all of this due to a Catastrophe? A seed cannot germinate without the proper soil.
The current turmoil in Kjerag only proves that people's beliefs had already changed, long before the Catastrophe itself. And the cause of all this is...
Enciodes.
Are you referring to... Mr. Enciodes Silverash, the Chairman of Karlan Trade?

For thousands of years, Kjerag has been spared from Catastrophe by our adherence to the order laid down by our god. However, the Vine-Bead Court is not the rigidly conservative institution that outsiders think.

Even in the era before power was restored to the three clans, records in the Vine-Bead Court's Hall of Writings confirm that the Court was involved in, and sometimes even instrumental to, the vast majority of changes across Kjerag's history.
However, we simply remind ourselves constantly of the wisdom and virtues She taught to us, ensuring that each step we take does not become counterproductive.
And, this order formed through the Vine-Bead Court has proven its reliability and stability over the long years.
Yet, when you don your skis and snowboards and zoom down our snow-capped peaks, how often do you turn back to look at those who lack the condition and ability to keep up?
No matter how much you might want to turn back and wait, the falling snow will obstruct your view until the exhilaration carried upon the wind engulfs your every thought.
You believe that Kjerag's current reforms are too radical...
Therefore, if anyone can be held responsible for the current state of Kjerag, it can only be Enciodes.
So... as an elder of the Vine-Bead Court, are you planning to make any changes in response to this situation?
No... I won't lightly say that I'll be making any changes myself...
I simply believe that... for thousands of years, the enemies of Kjeragandr have all vanished into the snowdrifts of history.
...The registration documents are all here. Please make sure this envelope makes its way to Mr. Edelweiss of Karlan Trade.
Don't worry. We were established by Karlan Trade and know Mr. Edelweiss very well. There won't be any mistakes.
Glad to hear it.
Elena turns her head somewhat nervously. After the Catastrophe, the people of Kjerag have largely preferred to remain at home, with one exception—the market.
Hundreds of people crowded together in residential areas, temples, stores, and street stalls, the sorrow on their faces matching the gloomy weather.
At the far end of the market, Joanna is gesturing to her with her hands, her mouth open as if beckoning for her to come quickly.
Be right there!
(Hushed voice) Ah—gotta keep a low profile.

Elena lowers her head and turns her face away, hurrying away against the flow of pedestrians. Countless Kjerag people brush past her, while another Rhine Lab employee squeezes into the crowd and follows the current.
(Hushed voice) Why should we choose the most crowded place...

People are forced to gather here in order to barter for necessities or to receive relief supplies. That they suppress their fear is a fact worn on their sleeves, many of them showing up in naught but simple, local attire.
Carolin instinctively pulls her coat in tighter, walking past the men and women, until she stops at the entrance to a quiet little temple.
She leans against the door frame, its paint worn and peeling, and hesitantly turns her head to look around, searching for someone else who also has an appointment.
Damn it, the bell already rang.
All I wanted was to find a place to continue my research. Why am I stuck doing this kind of work?
She pounds the wall next to her angrily, until suddenly, a hoarse question reaches her ears.
Oh lost lady, why do you treat the property of Kjeragandr in such a manner?
I'm sorry. How should I make amends?
There's no need for that. Set everything down, and come with me.
Should it be agreeable to you, please rest in the courtyard. I shall explain the profundity of the scriptures to you, so as to soothe your restless heart.
The old man unlocks the temple gate, and Caroline pauses apprehensively to look around before stepping inside. The devotee then follows her in and shuts the gate again.
...You're the one they mentioned. The one who can bring me out.
Choosing to meet up here is incredibly risky, is it not?
People are undoubtedly going to be anxious in the aftermath of a Catastrophe. Wandering around in sparsely-populated, open areas is only going to invite more suspicion.
Let's get down to business. We don't have time to waste.
Have you acquired all the intel?
I have, but it's not meant for you.
The Duke of Caster's men promised that once they took over the mine, I would be able to leave Kjerag via the internal railway.
I don't know what they told you, but now is the time for you to do your part.
The mine is currently in the handover period. Once the Victorians have finished their inspection, I will take you there to meet them.
I ask for your patience, madam. It is a precious virtue indeed.
I hope this is the last time...
Did you hear that?
Hear what?

Sounded a bit like thunder, how strange. Seemed to come from the mountains.
And also... Ah...
Achoo!
What's this? The tip of my nose feels all chilly... Huh?
That's weird. Why is it snowing again?
We're in the middle of the snowy season. What's so strange about that?
No, that can't be it. Even at this time of year, there are always signs before it starts snowing.
*sniff*
The girl lifts her head, her expression suddenly turning serious.
It's snowing, but it doesn't smell like snow. It's very strange...
An old man once said that the snow only loses its scent when something truly terrible is about to happen...
Is Kjeragandr... warning us again?
'Divine will' is less important than how heavy the snowfall is going to be, and how it will threaten our safety.
As for the 'scent of snow' you mentioned... the air temperature and humidity change before it snows, which may give off the illusion of a peculiar scent.
But, to treat this as some way of gauging the weather, or even as a divination of some sort is... well...
I frankly don't think it makes any sense.
No, no. The scent of snow is the scent of snow. Not some other smell.
Kjeragandr has given hunters a keen sense of smell and eyes that can see in the dark. On the snowfields, we know much more than you do!
Okay, then... I don't mean to question your personal experience, but I still have to act according to my own 'experience'.
As Elena speaks, she sets up the observation equipment and begins to carefully record the data.
The girl pouts discontentedly, looking at the device with a distinctively old-fashioned Kjerag look.
That lump of metal of yours has been beeping this entire time, and it's heavy too... We would've gotten here way sooner without having to lug it around...
But, it's how we can make sure that every step we take is safe and secure. After a Catastrophe, we must be extremely cautious inside Originium zones—
A sudden gust of wind rises, causing Elena to stumble, but Joanna catches her from behind just in the knick of time.
Thanks—
...Hmph.
The chilly, snowflake-laced wind sweeps over them, and all other noise ceases, save for the whistling wind and its sharp, biting cold.
How did the temperature drop so sharply all of a sudden? Is this...?
I know of a deserted village up ahead. Let's head over there and find a house to shelter in.
Enya, who has been lagging behind the group because she was flipping through her notebook, steps in between the two.
She stretches her palms out, her fingertips only slightly reddened from the cold.
Come. Take my hand.
The fresh snow is too slippery, and the terrain here is steep as well. It would be terrible if someone slipped and fell here.
Hmm... You're both hesitating?
Do you not want to hold my hands?
Enya... I mean, Your Holiness, I...
No, thank you.
One hand extends forward.
Hmph. I'm just doing this for Her Holiness.
Joanna turns her head away, but nevertheless places her hand within Enya's.
Phew... Thankfully, the temple is in a much better state than the other buildings because there still are people who come by occasionally to do repairs.
The three shake off the thick snow from their cloaks. They arrive at the village only briefly before the snow starts to come down heavily, fluttering down like feathers.
You two... Go ahead and rest. I'll see if the fireplace here can still be lit.
You can usually find a small woodshed behind the village temple. I'll go take a look.
Need my help?
...I'm good. You...
Didn't you say you were going to do the weather forecast? Just go do your thing!
I've already set it up. The measuring device will automatically gather data from our surrounding area, so I can help you carry—
I told you I'm good! Don't follow me...!
Before Elena can even stand up, Joanna already manages to feel out the small door behind the temple statue with practiced ease.
The layout of any Kjerag monastery is more or less the same. The girl rushes out back, and the door slams loudly behind her.
Did I do something to upset her...?
Maybe just a little, but not that much. Don't worry about it.
Joanna is a kind girl. Even when she's grumpy, she still wants you to be warm. Just like not long ago, when she protected me from assassins even though she despises me.
However, for many of Kjerag's villagers, your thoughts and words can hurt them, even if they are without malice.
I'm sorry... I was too rash...
That's not your fault, nor is it the fault of Kjerag's people. If anything is at fault... perhaps it is simply your differences.
Any sort of communication between people... is about these differences.
Even partners who work together in the lab, sharing the same ideas and goals, can still have disagreements.
Which is why... communication and understanding between people is so precious.
Enya folds her cloak and puts it aside, point to a snowflake on it that has not yet melted.
Oh, look at that.
...A snowflake?
The people of Kjerag sometimes use snowflakes that have not melted upon stepping indoors as a form of divination. These unbroken, branch-like snowflakes are a sign that our journey will be smooth.
According to folklore, the snow is a messenger protected by Kjeragandr, carrying important truths and omens.
It can predict the harvest, the hunt, the craftsman's hammer-fall, and even quarrels between friends and lovers.
Some may say that this iconic, tree-like hexagonal snowflake with well-defined branches is a sign of good fortune. But, others just as easily see within it a broken connection, an omen of disaster.
And so... regardless of what happens in the end, the fortune-teller always has something to say.
Worst-case scenario, they always say, 'the diviner you met was just bad; the movement of the stars had already revealed the truth long ago,' and use that to defend their authority.
You also seem quite knowledgeable about divination, Elena.
You could say I've had some run-ins with them... due to family circumstances.
In any case, most divination has nothing to do with revealing the truth. It's just... a form of self-delusion and sophistry.
A place where no people wish to go, has no roads leading to it.
If a problem does not exist, there will be no solution created to resolve it.
Fate is a fickle thing, just as your science should acknowledge that there are many things on this land that cannot be controlled or predicted.
On this great land, we are but tiny people trying to grasp a glimmer of the prophetic and the true.
Is this so different from the origins of 'scientific' discovery? Knowledge is what makes people feel safe and allows them to face an unreasonably uncertain future.
In a sense, coincidence, probability, and fate are all the same concept. Fate falls on many, just as this snowflake has fallen on my cloak.
I get it. Even placebos can be an efficacious type of medicine.
But... I've also seen many things in modern Kjerag.
In the past, I always tried to see such strong beliefs as a kind of cultural phenomenon; a ritual, a projection, a form of comfort-seeking...
But... what exactly are they to you people?
She asks the question in a soft, subdued voice. The wind outside the window has already subsided significantly.
Kjerag's Saintess looks up at the statue of Kjeragandr on the altar. Beneath a slab of stone engraved with messy markings, several brightly-colored figurines sit side-by-side.
They were exhibition goods produced around the time of the Lake Silberneherze ceremony. By the looks of things, someone brought them to this village temple as an offering to the reclusive Kjeragandr.
The clerics of the Vine-Bead Court often describe faith as walking into the darkness without a lamp, or leaping fearlessly into a silent ravine.
Everyone always seems to yearn for Her. Ascetics, devout believers, faithful followers of the Commandments—countless people endure innumerable hardships just to reach Her side.
But... for me...
She tries hard to recall, but the memories in her mind are now but struggling fragments, jumping around like frames from a movie, shrouded in a dark haze.
A strong emotion surges once more within Enya's heart.
Trouble—!
Joanna suddenly rushes in with a gasp, firewood in her arms, her cheeks flushed from running.
I just... I just saw, coming down the mountain... Lots of Victorian cars, heading for the mine!
Victorians? That mine is a joint operation with Kazimierz. What are they trying to do there...?
Have they already picked up our trail?
Don't panic just yet. Regardless of what business they have with the mine, we have to hurry.
Let's pack up our things and set out immediately. We need to be even more careful and discreet on the road ahead.

Just a second, please. I need to pack up my instrument first, and it...
Wait, how could this...?
What are you dawdling around for? Hurry and pack it up! Here, I'll help you...
What... is this?
This... 'Moderate Catastrophe Risk' written here... does it mean what I think it means?
Catastrophe...
Could there be another Catastrophe brewing...?
You know, it's real hard getting an audience with you these days.
...Enciodes.
I am truly sorry I was unable to attend the Matriarch's funeral.
Madam Sciurus, I am deeply saddened by what has befallen the Browntail clan. Please let me know if there is anything Clan Silverash can do to help.
Thanks, Enciodes.
But that's not what I came here to discuss.
Your visit this time, Madam Sciurus, must be to give me a chance to earn your trust.
Hmph... My sister once told me that you're least trustworthy when you lower your head like this.
I wonder about your opinion of me, then.
You don't have to beat around the bush. I don't like it.
My sister used to scold me for being impulsive, and for not always thinking things through before I speak or act.
I wanted to prove to her that I didn't need to hide under her wing, that I could do something for Clan Browntail.
But now, as new matriarch of the clan, there is no one left for me to prove anything to.
I understand how you feel. I... also have two younger sisters.
And you think they're both smarter than me, huh? Both Ensia, and the Saintess...
You think too little of yourself, Madam Sciurus.
However, I do think they share your persistence and determination.
Is Ensia okay? I heard... she was in an accident.
Thank you for your concern. She is safe and sound, and has already returned home.
You always keep that calm and composed demeanor.
What I want to know is... these attacks on Kjerag, including what happened to my sister... Did you know about them ahead of time?
So this is the real reason you decided to visit me today.
Enciodes, my sister had been helping you this entire time, isn't that right?
I wasn't totally in the dark about it, but it wasn't until her passing that I learned...
Your plans—for Victoria, Rhine Lab, and what Monch has been investigating... There's too much I don't know anything about.
Don't try to deny it. Don't lie to me, Enciodes. You have to know the truth behind her murder.
If you truly believe I had a hand in the Matriarch's death—
No... That's not what I mean.
I don't know what Ratatos was plotting with you, but I know for sure... that whatever it was, it was to protect Kjerag.
So... I will follow in her footsteps... Whatever Clan Browntail's affairs may be, I will take responsibility for them.
Which is why... I need you to tell me exactly what happened to her—
My apologies, Sir Enciodes, but they were determined to break in no matter what—
To what do I owe the honor, for you all to barge into my home like this?
Sir Enciodes, why did Karlan Trade transfer all its shares in the Kjerag-Kazimierz joint mining operation to Victoria without authorization?
Where did this news come from?
It is an observable fact—the mines have completely been taken over by the Victorians.

Great Elder Adso believes that such actions by Karlan Trade constitute illegal collusion with a foreign power, and as such, the Vine-Bead Court has no choice but to exercise its authority to intervene.
We require that you report to the Vine-Bead Court immediately to explain yourself.
If I understand this correctly, the Vine-Bead Court means to formally interrogate Clan Silverash?
The people of Kjerag are uneasy enough as it is. The last thing we want is to see this conflict escalate further.
However, Sir Enciodes, as the head of the clan, if you still believe you bear responsibility for Kjerag, please go to the Vine-Bead Court with us to discuss the next steps.
Very well. I have no reason to refuse.
However, if I am to explain the situation, I will need some time to prepare the relevant documents. Please allow me that.
Furthermore, I still have some things to discuss with Madam Sciurus, so I would like to ask for your patience.
Of course, Sir Enciodes.
Madam Sciurus, thank you for taking the time to look after Ensia. I would like to formally invite you to be a guest of Clan Silverash.
I would also like to thank you for your concern in person, while Ensia is present as well.
Wha...?
It would have been more courteous to have sent the invitation to you in a formal capacity.
But given the hasty nature of this matter, the best I can do is to throw something together by hand. I do hope you'll forgive me.
We look forward to you gracing us with your presence.
Before his 'invitee' can respond, Enciodes picks up a pen and paper from the table, scribbles down a few lines, then hands the 'invitation' over.
Sciurus takes it. There is only an address on the piece of paper, hardly an invitation at all.
Thank you for the invitation.
I'll be there on time.

lines.st: To Mr. Edelweiss of Karlan Trade:
I hope this letter finds you well.
At the time you are read this, I am yet to receive sitrep authorization from Rhine Lab, so please take note—
This letter does not, in any capacity, serve as an agreement or treaty between Rhine Lab and Karlan Trade. Rather, it only represents my personal analysis of the recent phenomena in Kjerag.
My specimen comes from the sound waves collected by the observation station at 3:19 AM on December 7, 1101.
I can say for certain that at that time, an unidentified object fell from the sky onto Mount Karlan.
Though the chain of evidence definitively linking the phenomena is still missing, a Catastrophe did in fact occur 32 hours later.
I do not intend to establish a causal link between these two things. If this crash is a singular event in history, then so too is the Catastrophe.
What I AM preparing to do is to confirm whether or not there is an empirical connection between these two events.
Additionally, do forgive my frankness, but if the hypothesis that these two events are linked does bear out, the outcome for Kjerag is not optimistic—
Whatever factor that has protected Kjerag from Catastrophes over the millennia may no longer be effective, or at least, be in an unusable state.
I would, of course, love to write this off as mere conjecture. But nevertheless, I also hope that Karlan Trade will be adequately mindful of the potential risks moving forward.
Carolin, an insider with more detailed information, has likely made similar inferences, but I am in the dark as to what her objectives are at this moment.
I will strive to complete this research while maintaining an objective and rational perspective, and would greatly appreciate Karlan Trade's support in my efforts.
Respectfully, Elena Urbica.

lines.x: Come in.
I was just looking for you, Director.
Take this letter over to Enciodes. Classify it top secret.
Yes, sir. Right away.
Any other news?
Yes—the observation post previously dispatched to the mining site has sent back a sighting report.
A convoy bearing Victorian markings is approaching the outskirts of the mine, but the specific origins are unknown.
Matterhorn led his clansmen there ahead of time. He said that he would try to stall for as much time as possible and await your judgment.
The Victorians would've come sooner or later. Looks like we won't have to wait any longer.
Prepare vehicles as soon as possible. Matterhorn may not be able to hold them off for long.
Tell him to let the other party make the first move, and prioritize observation. I will arrive shortly.
...I'm warning you, don't get in our way, if you know what's good for you.
I am simply following orders. The area ahead is a restricted warehouse, and nobody is allowed to enter without Karlan Trade's permission.
Has no one informed you that House Caster has acquired all of Kazimierz's rights to this site?
To the best of my knowledge, the handover process is still underway. We were only notified by Kazimierz yesterday.
Which is why we have to inspect all the assets in advance. If you have an complaints, you can take them up with Her Grace the Duke.
Now, listen to your betters and open the warehouse door post-haste.
Finally, someone worth talking to.
Mr. Gnosis Edelweiss of Karlan Trade.
It seems the introduction I've prepared will be going to waste, good sir.
Of course. Naturally, you also understand who I'm representing here.

Due to your mismanagement and the resultant substantial financial losses, Her Grace the Duke has decided to dismiss some key employees of Karlan Trade employed at this site, including the former executive manager.
To this, I have no objection. I will cooperate fully during this handover process.
Excellent. Now then, Mr. Edelweiss, please open the warehouse door.
...However, on this point alone, I cannot comply.
What Matterhorn said is true. There indeed are regulations pertaining to this confidential warehouse. I humbly ask for yours and Her Grace's understanding.
Based on the information I have, the security clearance level on this warehouse was raised just recently.
What do you say to this, Mr. Edelweiss?
Allow me to explain.
This warehouse is mainly used to store various kinds of industrial waste, but recently, the purification system and negative pressure ventilation system both malfunctioned.
We temporarily raised the security clearance level and strictly restricted access in the interim before professionals can handle the situation.
I fully understand Her Grace's concern about the assets acquired, but for your safety, could I ask that this inspection be postponed?
I accept the risks at hand. Just open the door.
All else aside, this is Kjerag's mine.
Should pollutants leak, or any other dangerous situation arises, Kjerag does not rule out the right to intervene through administrative measures.
Before you offer to take responsibility, I suggest you contact Her Grace the Duke once more.
You're...
Good to see you again, Mr. Matterhorn.
Pardon me, Father. I'm afraid I'll have to ask you to wait at reception for now. I currently have business with our diplomatic guest.
Mr. Edelweiss, I was expressly invited here by the Victorians.
Since when is the Vine-Bead Court so concerned with temporal affairs?
Today, both the clan leadership and the general public are dissatisfied with the way Karlan Trade has been engaging with Victoria.
The Great Elder has summoned Sir Enciodes to an inquiry into the matter; perhaps, you will also need to report to the Vine-Bead Court soon.
Why, then, would a cleric feel a need to accept an invitation to come here before that?
I wish to ensure a smooth handover process, and hope that Karlan Trade is willing to cooperate to the fullest extent.
Please open the warehouse door, Mr. Edelweiss.
Given that power has been restored by the three clans, perhaps the Vine-Bead Court would be more representative of Kjerag's 'administrative measures'?
Matterhorn, open the door.
The door slowly opens, and the Victorian ambassador is caught unaware by the dust that diffuses from within. He squints and fans the air in front of him with his hand.
There is neither the smell of industrial waste nor the texture of Originium dust. The ambassador's eyes gradually adjust to the vast, unobstructed warehouse, within which is—
...Nothing but scrap metal?
As I stated, there is nothing here but industrial waste.
Please leave as soon as you've finished your inspection. Staying here for an extended period of time would be detrimental to your health.
Everyone, stay put. I'm conducting the inspection myself.

As soon as he enters the warehouse, he feels the vast space around him dilating in all directions, the steel frames stretching straight to the ceiling surrounded by scrap metal of all shapes and sizes.
There's no way such a large warehouse was set up just to store these things. It's far too pretentious.
He approaches the bottom of one of the shelves and reaches out to rummage through it with no particular aim in mind. The clanging of metal echoes through the warehouse.
(Hushed voice) Wait, this is...

(Hushed voice) It's still half-intact, and I can still make out the insignia of Silverash's private army, the 'Tschäggättä'. Were these originally weapons and gear not improperly disposed of after being thrown in the shredder...?
An idea bubbles up, and he keeps looking elsewhere. Though Karlan Trade has been quite meticulous, a few clues are bound to leak through. Of this, he is certain.
(Hushed voice) Replicas of Victoria's newest equipment, as well as large-caliber naval gun parts...
(Hushed voice) All failures, unable to break through the technical barrier...
The army designations. The insignias. Many other terms in the Kjeragi language. He quietly memorizes them all.
Sir! Are you done yet?
...Yes, I am.
The ambassador returns to the group.
I've more or less seen what I needed to see, Mr. Edelweiss.
I will summarize the entire situation and faithfully report it to the Duke.
Sir, the internal railway in the mining site has just been cleared, and you haven't had time to inspect it yet.
Does Victoria also need to use that internal railway?
Yes, we've contacted a number of suppliers for quotes. Using the internal railway can significantly reduce transportation costs.

Father, is the Vine-Bead Court aware of this? If you are so keen to get involved in diplomatic affairs, Karlan Trade hopes that you will also be willing to verify any business entities that wish to join.
Gnosis's concern interrupts the thoughts of the Vine-Bead Court envoy, who then looks away from the railway.
...That would require the Great Elder's input.
I understand.
By Kjeragandr, I ask that the Vine-Bead Court not make things difficult for Enciodes. I will remain at Karlan Trade's headquarters, awaiting your summons.
By Kjeragandr.
...You've finally come.
Enciodes.
Naturally, I would not dare refuse an invitation from the Vine-Bead Court... Great Elder Adso.
If I remember correctly, it shouldn't take very long to get from the Silverash domain to the Vine-Bead Court.
To be summoned to the Vine-Bead Court is a matter of great importance. As I stand accused of improper dealings with Victoria, I had to make ample preparations to prove my innocence.
Without further ado, Sir Enciodes, please come inside so that we may discuss this further.
One moment, please.
A large convoy of vehicles pulls up to the center of the Vine-Bead Court.
Silverash... What is the meaning of this?
Are you not investigating the ties between Karlan Trade and Victoria?

I had someone compile all the documents related to the last decade of business dealings between Karlan Trade and Victoria. It is quite the voluminous collection, so much so that we had to fill several trucks.
Great Elder Adso, if it interests you, please take your time to read through it.
Good... Very good.
Unload everything, then tell your people to leave.
I'm afraid I can't do that.
What is the meaning this, Enciodes?
It is true that as of late, Kjerag has had to weather great turmoil. Undoubtedly, Victoria has been at the heart of many of these incidents.
Indeed, our troops have discovered evidence of contact between the Vine-Bead Court and Victorian spies.
Empty accusations. Without proof, what right do you have to investigate the Vine-Bead Court?
Investigate? I would never dream of such a thing. However, the Vine-Bead Court has accused me of 'colluding with Victoria', and so I am here.
By my command, nobody is to leave the Vine-Bead Court today until Great Elder Adso and I reach a conclusion.
Yessir!
Now then, Great Elder Adso.
...We've never talked like this before.
I have hardly even seen you on my past visits to the Vine-Bead Court, let alone spoken with you.
I've heard from many clerics that you spent years in seclusion, studying the scriptures in the Hall of Writings and never concerning yourself with temporal affairs.
It seems you've also come to handle the affairs of the Vine-Bead Court with ease.
The only thing I've done over the years is ensure that Kjerag follows the path She ordained.

Moreover, we stand at a critical juncture concerning our survival. Should the Vine-Bead Court stand by and watch you drag Kjerag into the abyss, allowing Kazimierz, Victoria, or any other foreign power to devour us?
You are not the king of Kjerag, Sir Enciodes.
Thank you for the admonition, Great Elder. If Kjerag were to have a king, it could only be Her.
If there are diplomatic errors the Vine-Bead Court wishes to correct, then Karlan Trade will do our best to cooperate.
However, there is one question that puzzles me, for which I seek your guidance.
How exactly is the Vine-Bead Court negotiating with Victoria?
Is it using all of Kjerag as a bargaining chip?
Elder!
(Whispering) We just took a look around. Silverash's troops have already blocked the way down the mountain.
(Whispering) We, we'll go check the back door and—
The secret passage?
I wouldn't bother with that unless you're keen on searching for Tschäggättä.
Ahh!
Don't be alarmed. By Kjeragandr, I have no intention of attacking the Vine-Bead Court.
Tell everyone outside to rest where they are. No one is to move without my order.
...Enciodes, you accuse the Vine-Bead Court of betraying Kjerag to Victoria. Where is the evidence for your claim?
Please be patient, Great Elder. It will take some time for the evidence to be delivered here.
In other words, you came empty-handed yet still chose to resort to violence against us.
You should know what the price is for doing this, head of the Silverash clan.
Look down at the people beneath the mountain. Your elite troops might be able to block off the Vine-Bead Court, but you cannot fight all of Kjerag.

I am willing to give you time... But if you cannot produce conclusive evidence before sundown, I'm afraid that the reputation of Karlan Trade, and even that of the entire Silverash name, will be in tatters.
You will become untouchables throughout the land of Kjerag.
Indeed. I am, of course, well-aware of the consequences of my actions.

What I do not know, however, is what it would mean for Kjerag if the Vine-Bead Court is found to be colluding with an enemy that assassinated leaders from the three clans, and was caught in the act of espionage.
I will be with you to witness that moment, Elder.
...I know exactly what you're waiting for.
But, time is not on your side, Sir Enciodes.
Let us in! We were invited by the Vine-Bead Court to participate in the blessing ceremony, and Silverash's goons have no right to stop us!
Throughout her illustrious career, The Black Knight was unshakeable, even by the most fearsome opponents.
But now, looking down at the teeming crowds crammed onto the narrow mountain road, she feels utterly helpless for the first time.
Why haven't we seen a single cleric here? What is Silverash trying to pull?!
Please calm down, everyone. The Vine-Bead Court... has encountered some extenuating circumstances, and Sir Enciodes is assisting with the investigation.
We came here on an invitation, and you're telling us that there are 'extenuating circumstances' now that we're here? Cut the crap!
A man squeezes through the crowd, but as soon as he takes a step forward, he is met with Degenbrecher's cold glare and has no choice but to retreat, muttering under his breath.
Sir Enciodes will issue an explanation to the public after things have settled down. Please, I ask that you all return home for now.
...Ma'am, be careful—
I'm fine, Degenbrecher. I can stand on my own...
We all trust you. We know that you're a daughter of Kjerag and would never bring us harm.
So, can you tell this old woman exactly what is happening with the Vine-Bead Court? What do you know about this whole thing?
A vehicle winds its way along the mountain road; outside the window, dense dark clouds are gathering, mingling.
The window rolls down, and a few snowflakes laced with unidentified impurities slide into the car before disappearing without a trace.
The signal's a little better now.
According to our informant, Victoria and the people from the Vine-Bead Court have left the mining site, and have not made any further moves.
But given this weather, we can expect more heavy snow... It'll take a lot longer to get back to headquarters than expected.
Compared to THAT, this little hiccup can hardly be considered beyond our expectations.
Are you referring to those Victorians from earlier?
They got the intel they wanted, but so did we.
I mean the Vine-Bead Court.
The Vine-Bead Court, huh...
The fact that they'd show up here instead of calming the populace is rather strange indeed...
It's evident that they were completely unprepared for the situation, and their deliberate attempts to hide their schemes are easily seen through.
If some of them are willing to play cover-up for the Victorians—
Then, it's only a matter of time until the mask comes off.
It's nearly time.
Turn back to the mining site.
Are you trying to...?
The exit ways there are now open, making it a good place to leave the country.
As you just heard, Enciodes has already surrounded the Vine-Bead Court with his men. He has deliberately made a big show of things to alert the enemy.
Those hiding in Kjerag will definitely be itching to seize the moment.
Our undercover agent friend we've been tracking seems to have a particular fondness for this kind of stuff, and should be arriving in the vicinity soon.
Moreover, I suspect she's not the only one either.
Instead of looking for her, we should just let her come to us.
Gather everyone together and keep a close eye on the entrance to the mining site. Pay extra attention to—
The people who helped her escape.
...While you were on your way, the clergy throughout the city were already inviting people to come up the mountain for the ceremony.
I wonder, how long Degenbrecher can hold them back?
Did the Vine-Bead Court stoop to these desperate measures knowing I would not point my weapons at the people?
It's true that I'm using them, just as you did to Arctosz and Ratatos during the Holy Hunt a few years ago.
Perhaps I overestimated you, Sir Enciodes. Even if you're hungry for power, you should be less obvious about it.
Before you commit a grave error, I am willing to announce to the public that you've come to the Vine-Bead Court to capture the Victorian assassin.
If you truly thought highly of me in any capacity, you would not think that I am only 'seeking power'.
All I care about is the future of the land of Kjerag.
So, a violent coup is the best path forward?
Yes.
Because our Victorian friend sits in a position that cannot be reached except through violence.
Hahahaha! What you say is true, Sir Enciodes.
The unarmed Vine-Bead Court, driven to despair by the descendants of Victorians and their acolytes, helpless to defend themselves even when thieves cry thief in front of them.
Behavior determines identity better than blood.
It is a fact that since the attack on Ensia, people from the Vine-Bead Court have been falling over themselves to back Victoria.
If you are so confident you know what I'm waiting for—
The cleric and the Victorian spy caught red-handed together. Be it for the general public or the three clans, the rest speaks for itself.
I know no more about the conduct of these clerics than you do.
Even if the Duke of Caster were to come here herself, she would only tell you that I firmly rejected her offer.
That is nothing more than an excuse. A failure to control your subordinates means you are equally responsible for them, and the same applies to Karlan Trade.
...What you suggest is that the clergy of the Vine-Bead Court indulge in pleasure and collude with foreigners, while I remain oblivious and allow them to sell out Kjerag?
You and Victoria share many of the same delusions; in reality, the Vine-Bead Court has only ever wanted to use this as a pretext to expel Victoria and their true ally—
Enciodes Silverash.
However, the reality is that they ended up aligning with Victoria in practice—
All the clergy who've associated with Victoria have sworn an oath of death after you are expelled.
Their bones will nourish Kjerag's future, and they will leave no regrets behind.
So, what you propose is that Kjerag should purge itself, down to those who hunt Victorian spies to this day, those who the Duke of Caster considers enemies...
And THAT is the only way to defeat Victoria?
There is nobody around. A sudden gust of wind suddenly howls through the unfamiliar courtyard.
Carolin continues to stir around her piping-hot cup of savory milk tea, the spoon continuing to clink against the cup.
She shudders in anticipation, either due to the drink or her impending evacuation.
She stares at the swirling tea and strokes the cup, completely ignorant to the fact that the cup is engraved with Kjerag's mountain ridges.
Her thoughts are interrupted by a knock on the door.
Who is it?
...Stay back!
Sorry to keep you waiting, young lady of Victoria.
It's you...
Please pardon my intrusion, but due to the situation changing, you will be transferred to a new destination ahead of schedule.
What?
...What happened?
I only know the gist of the Vine-Bead Court's decision.
A 'gist' isn't good enough. We need to have a concrete plan of action, or else...
I will take full responsibility for the operation until you have safely left Kjerag's borders.
All you need to do is follow the instructions. As for how to leave and who will help you—
Don't overly concern yourself about such things.
Kjeragandr witnesses all, and She shall walk with us.
Stop trying to fool me with your vague and nondescript dogma...
By Kjeragandr.
In other words, I've become your responsibility now, right?
I am but a follower of Her will.
Fine. You have your beliefs, and I have mine.
But since we'll be working together, let me remind you that if this operation fails, your side will also be exposed.
That's right.
Does that not worry you at all?
The cleric simply smiles gently and takes a garment out from his bag.
No, young lady.
All I need is faith.
You can also place your faith in us. As long as we are of one heart, we can overcome our fear.
Cleric of the Vine-Bead Court.
I don't think we need to discuss this any further.
Instead, let's focus on something useful, like when we leave.
My apologies, young lady. This is a cleric's robe that I sewed myself. Please put it on for now.
This journey will take you past many people and countless eyes. If you encounter someone who comes up to you seeking prayer or answers, do not try to please them. Stay silent, and avoid them.
I will walk with you, no matter what dangers we may face.
Color me curious. Why do people pray to your god for protection even after a Catastrophe?
Just in case.
Believe me, young lady. You know too little about Kjerag.
Now, please pack your belongings. I will wait for you outside.
The door opens, and a dry, cold wind rushes in, lashing at the cleric's neck and face.
An old dagger slips from his bag, and he nonchalantly bends down to pick it up.
He knows exactly what this amulet, forged for him by his father who has returned to Kjeragandr, will ultimately be used for.
By Kjeragandr.
May this devout body be blessed, and may it spread Your holy will.
They brought excellent seeds to Kjerag as well as machinery... The Vine-Bead Court has never denied these contributions.
To be frank, I have never seen anyone so complicated in my already-withering life...
Hero.
This does nothing to dispel my doubts.
Compared to these acts, the Vine-Bead Court seems only to recognize friend and foe based on where they come from.
Then, allow me to ask you a question first, Sir Enciodes.
In your heart of hearts, what exactly is Kjerag?
...I have already answered this through my actions.
Victorian-style buildings. Victorian-style weapons. Victorian-mandated laws.
Is this your answer?
Unless we choose to seclude ourselves deeper within the mountains, shunning both external disturbances and any further advancement.
Otherwise, you might even be able to see things from Laterano or Leithanien one day.
Until one day, the people of Kjerag believe in nothing but Originium and money, unless faith itself can be sold.
Once the shell of Kjeragandr is ripped open, the people shall no longer hold any reverence for eternity. They will only chase after their own values and desires.
They will hate the strong because in this new 'theater', the more one abandons his morality, the stronger he becomes.
They will hate the weak because the weak will come to occupy the crevices of pity and charity.
Nihilism will become the most popular ideology; the people's hearts will become empty and detached, and they will take pride in this emptiness.
Your ideals are slaughtering the people of Kjerag, turning Victoria's present into our future.
...The Kjeragandr that the Vine-Bead Court worships is far more fragile than the one I worship.
If all it takes to overthrow our sacred beliefs are a few foreign tools, then how has Mount Karlan stood to this day?
How could it be that foreign tools can cover our mountains and rivers, yet foreign ideas can never be touched?
If this is what you believe, then you must also admit that what can conquer the body can also conquer the soul.
Born into a dark and hopeless era, you must race faster than the clouds to avoid being swallowed by darkness.
Faith should not drive people to become accustomed to the darkness, but rather unwaveringly affirm the yearning for light.
Yet, the more pious one is, the more destitute he becomes. In due time, he will see piety as a noose, and begin to hate the history of faith itself.
Which is why those who adhered to the teachings of Kjeragandr were ridiculed as pedantic and ignorant.
Without turning on the tap, there is no clean drinking water. Without applying chemical fertilizers, there is no fertile farmland.
You leveled the mountains and forests to build your factories, then sent the displaced back to the ruins of their homes to either sell their labor or die.
The lives you've improved for some, are fed by the happiness you've stolen from others.
O eloquent grandmaster, I ask you—how should we deal with the 'stragglers' left in your wake?
...You are right. It is a heavy responsibility that I must accept.
However, the Vine-Bead Court only fantasizes about incremental change while continuing to regard doing nothing as a virtue.
Such people have no right to judge me.
I have never sought to block Kjerag's progress. My only responsibility is to pull the reins on self-important tourists before things spiral out of control.
You're moving too quickly, Sir Enciodes.
The way to format the Kjeragandr, the way to punctuate prayer passages, which hand to raise during rituals... are debates that can last for centuries.
Elder of Clan Browntail, you have both the talent and the platform, and yet you only yearn for the solace of the other shore.
Yet when you hide away in the Hall of Writings and philosophize from your armchair, do you know anything about where Kjerag is actually headed?
What I know... is that Kjerag would never let an apostate take the helm. And, I have never been one to 'philosophize from my armchair'.
You are not the first apostate I've dealt with.
What memories come to mind when you hear the names, Olafur and Elizabeth Silverash?
What...?
This bloody weather again... Damn it, why's there a traffic jam in a place like this?
Hey, you in the back. The road's about to get real bumpy.
If you want to be able to stand after getting out of the truck, I'd recommend taking a nap now and conserving your energy.
Inside the rear compartment, the lights flicker as the truck sways. The passengers trying to escape sit upright on their haystacks, eyes tightly shut.
Shit, what's that thing in front of me?!
Urgh...
An oil slick spills across the middle of the road, and in front of the stopped truck is a line of fully-armed men.
The engine emits a strange, strained noise, and the windows are splattered with paint.
Inside the driver's seat, the Victorian tightly grips the steering wheel.
I've never tried to hide where I come from, even though it's something that others have long forgotten.
...Luca Browntail. Ratatos and Sciurus's grandfather. My cousin.
In order to achieve the same goal, we accepted different fates—to safeguard Kjerag's order through the Tri-Clan Council and the Vine-Bead Court.
Though known for his ruthlessness, he still harbored fantasies about the Silverashes until the very end.
He said: 'A peaceful resolution is never impossible, as long as the couple are willing to make concessions.'
...But, I have never lived in fantasy.
That train accident...
Hahaha... Is that what you believe? Or, did Ratatos make you believe it?
How many clergymen and devotees do you think are out there among the commoners?
If the masses wish for Kjeragandr to punish the apostates, then I, serving the Vine-Bead Court, shall deliver on those wishes.
...Sir Enciodes!
The man who killed your parents is right in front of you.
Those who commit murder will be punished twice over by Kjeragandr. This crippled body has lost any meaning for me long ago!
I grant you permission to dispose of me as you wish for reasons private and public, not for treason, but for murder. Arrest me here and put me on trial!
Let the people of Kjerag see just how sinful it is to kill an apostate!
The old man wobbles to his feet, his withered fingers clenching into fists as if using all his strength. Suddenly, he stretches his arms out before Enciodes.
Like a sinner waiting to have his confession heard, his cloudy eyes shine with a glimmer of light, as if seeking to quench decades of old grudges with another's anger.
Enciodes slowly closes his eyes, his fingertips wrapping tightly around his cane.
If you're trying to provoke me into acting out of line, I'm afraid your efforts are in vain.
I won't lay a finger on you here...
All I can do is ensure that you receive a fair trial...
Hurry it up! You in the back, keep up!
Our intel indicates that the target should be around here. Search carefully, and leave no stone unturned!
After that woman left the room, an unknown amount of time passed, to the point that time itself seemed alien to Monch.
Only the stinging pain of the ropes tightly binding her reminds her that she has not yet lost consciousness.
Finally, a hopeful sound comes from beyond the door, and Monch musters what might she has left to shout out.
I'm... over here...
I'm—
But try as she might, her parched throat can hardly produce a sound.
She laboriously crawls towards the door.
It opens.
Monch!
Madam... Why are you here...?

We've been searching for you ever since the Catastrophe hit! That damned Silverash, pretentious bastard. Thought they actually had some useful intel... but in the end, I still ended up having to do all the legwork myself to find you!
How're you doing? You hurt anywhere?
Wait, your leg—
I'm... fine...
Madam, listen to me...
The researcher I'm tracking... She's from Victoria, but more important... she's got ties, to Columbia...
What they're really after is...
Monch! MONCH!
Along the narrow mountain path, the convoy slowly plods onward.
After the incident's sudden outbreak, no shortage of people left their cars to observe the conflict's epicenter.
Turn off the engine. Get out.
What do you think you're doing? Who the hell are you people?
I... I'm an industrial investigator dispatched from Victoria! This is illegal obstruction of official business!
You've damaged our property, endangered Victorian lives, and almost even caused an accident! Mark my words, the Vine-Bead Court will hear of this!
Never should've come to this bloody shithole...
...Are you done?
Did you get all the pictures?
Yessir!
This is no time for you to try to twist the narrative, Victorian. Just cooperate.
Open up the rear compartment.
Unwilling to cooperate? Fine by me. You're more than welcome to conserve your energy to fend for yourselves before the court–if you're cold, I can offer you a blanket.
Matterhorn waves his hand, and the guard pries open the rear compartment without a word.
Seated upright upon a haystack there is an old cleric.
He brushes the dust off his body and walks out.
It is an emotionless affair, hardly amounting to courage.
The old cleric gazes upon the blinding brightness before him, and recollects the scenes from before.
Some ten minutes ago...
Why'd the truck stop?
Have we arrived?
Carolin lowers her voice.
...Did something happen?
Stay calm, young lady.
...Did they find us?
I didn't expect to find you here, Cleric of the Vine-Bead Court.
Let's continue this conversation elsewhere. This place is less than ideal.
You are as hasty as always, young master of Clan Edelweiss.
Honorable friends of Clan Silverash, I apologize for disturbing your work during this inclement weather.
I am more than glad to go along with your arrangements.
Looks like the one we're after isn't here.
I do believe this is where we part ways, young lady from Victoria.
Are you getting off?
No, I'll stay here. Just keep walking forward, and don't look back.
The entrance isn't far off. Silverash's people will surely notice this truck. Get out of here before they find you.
By Kjeragandr... trust me.
The old cleric helps Carolin up.
I'll do as you said.
But, you'll also be exposed.
That, too, is Kjeragandr's will. What I cannot change, I shall therefore accept.
I ask only for Her forgiveness.
I can't say I understand what your beliefs truly are—but maybe some day, I will.
I respect your beliefs, so I will also try to find a way to live in peace. Best of luck to you, sir.
Carolin gets out of the truck and runs without stopping, in a direction leading far, far from Kjerag.
Shedding her stained cleric's robes, she shivers from the cold.
Alone she walks through the vast, pale mountainscape.
Until in the distance, a dark tunnel entrance beckons to her.
Through the telescope's lens, Gnosis sees the mysterious scientist step into the shadows.
Arrest these two and have them sent straight to the Vine-Bead Court.
I've brought one.
I'm afraid I cannot grant your wish, Great Elder.
Upon hearing that long-awaited voice, Enciodes stands up and walks past the old man who remains frozen like a wooden sculpture.
Gnosis executed the plan. The Tschäggättä deployed immediately on his signal.
Before the presence of these wraiths, the clerics could do nothing but stumble back into their abodes, their tender faces affixed in fear.
I, I'm one of the Vine-Bead Court's clerics! You can't treat me like this—
Shut up and stop resisting!
...Master Frey is here too! Please, help me!
The one just brought in was the once-arrogant envoy of the Vine-Bead Court. His gaze sweeps over the shouting cleric, and he shakes his head.
Father Frey, I believe I can now explain your actions.
Victoria's agents conspired with the Vine-Bead Court's clerics to smuggle spies out of the country through the mine railway, but were caught red-handed thanks to Karlan Trade.
And before that, you were assisting the Victorians with scouring the mining site. Premeditated ill-intent.
And lest you forget, you protected the suspect following Ratatos's funeral.
You will formally be put on trial for these crimes.
By Kjeragandr... Forgive him, for he knows not of what he speaks––
It is a fact that spies have infiltrated the Vine-Bead Court. I urge you to not resist. Cooperation with our investigation is the only path to leniency.
Take Elder Great Adso, Father Frey, and the other elders of the Vine-Bead Court away for questioning. The other clerics are to remain there, awaiting their summons.
Yessir!
Now then, Great Elder Adso––
Silverash, do you think you've won?
'Those who live by the blade will find their arrogance short-lived.'
I wonder, just how long will your arbitrary reign of terror last?
Without making any unnecessary movements, Adso gives a slight nod to the crowd, then slowly walks to the door.
Great Elder! What should we do?!
...Survive.
The rest is already written in the scriptures. I have nothing else to say.
You are Her servants, and whatever happens, you must act according to your conscience.
Take him away.
By Kjeragandr, may the guilty receive their fair trial.

This is an abandoned mining shaft—the torches and tunnel lights don't work anymore. Bend down carefully, and watch your head...
If we wrap around from this side, we should just be able to make it...
Urgh!
Watch out!
...Come over first. Watch your step.
Joanna shifts her feet, and several rocks tumble down the cave, the sounds of dislodged pebbles echoing through the cracked crevices as they fall. She shudders.

So deep... You'd probably turn into a stain on the ground if you fell from this height...
Damn those Victorians for causing trouble in the mines... Else, we wouldn't have to come this way...
Huh? Saintess, what are you looking at?
